commitMessage: Add interior holes to Iron Snake boards (JOE-6)

topics.title: Add interior obstacle holes to Iron Snake boards
topics.trigger: A customer requested that Iron Snake boards have holes in the middle — interior gaps the snake must navigate around, where colliding with one is fatal, just like hitting the outer wall.
topics.response:
- Added a `carveHoles()` function in `src/iron-snake.ts` that punches small interior voids into an already-valid solid board shape. Holes are validated before being committed: each candidate is rejected if it breaks single-component connectivity (which would make some food unreachable) or creates a corridor narrower than the existing minimum-width threshold. Both validators were reused verbatim from the base shape generator rather than written fresh.
- Wired hole carving into both board generators: `generateIronSnakeBoard` (level 1) carves after the solid shape passes validation; `growIronSnakeBoard` (level transitions) builds a protected-cell set covering every snake cell plus its orthogonal neighbors before carving, so a hole can never open under a snake or immediately in front of a head when play resumes.
- Hole count and maximum footprint size scale with the Iron Snake level: early boards get one or two small single-cell gaps; later boards get several larger cavities (up to roughly 4×4 cells by level 8–10), providing a natural difficulty ramp without any separate tuning pass.
topics.decisions:
- **Reusing existing invariant checkers as hole validators**: Rather than writing new safety logic, the hole-carving pass reuses the same connectivity check and minimum-width morphological open that already guard the base shape. This means any hole that survives is automatically guaranteed to be a genuine interior gap with adequate clearance — the minimum-width check rejects holes placed within one cell of another wall, so thin corridors and edge bites are impossible without any extra code.
- **Anchoring placement attempts on known playable cells**: Random placement across the full bounding grid wastes most of the attempt budget on off-board positions, since the playable blob covers only about 57% of the grid. Anchoring each attempt on an actual on-board cell and requiring the entire footprint to be surrounded by playable neighbors (the "interior only" constraint) was the change that made hole placement reliable on small early boards, where deep-interior positions are a minority.
- **Level-scaled count and size rather than fixed tuning constants**: Tying hole count and maximum footprint to the current level means the difficulty increase is automatic as the arena grows across levels, and no separate difficulty curve needs to be maintained. The play-test feedback during the session (holes should be randomly larger and more numerous at later levels) was the direct prompt for this approach over the original area-only scaling.
topics.todo: The "Fell into a hole!" death message was not visually confirmed firing during automated verification — bots avoid holes, so the message could not be triggered in the headless run. A quick manual play-through to confirm the message appears on a hole collision is still outstanding.

recap:
Iron Snake boards now contain interior holes — dark voids surrounded by the playable surface that the snake must navigate around. Colliding with a hole is immediately fatal, food never spawns inside one, and the bot correctly routes around them. The holes grow in number and size as the player advances through levels: early boards have one or two small single-cell gaps, while later boards host several larger cavities, giving the mode a natural difficulty ramp without any manual tuning.

On level transitions, holes are carved after the new board shape is finalized, and the area immediately around the snake is always protected, so a hole can never appear under the snake or directly in its path the moment play resumes. The death screen now distinguishes between hitting the outer wall and falling into an interior hole, in both single-player and two-player modes.

references.title: Iron Snake: boards with holes in the middle

e2eTestGuide.title: Interior holes appear on a new Iron Snake board
e2eTestGuide.preconditions: Have the game open and ready to start a new session.
steps: Open the game and select Iron Snake mode.
Start a new game at level 1.
Look at the board that appears before the snake starts moving.
Check whether there are any small gaps or empty patches sitting in the middle of the playable area — not along the outer edge, but fully surrounded by playable cells on all sides.
Start a new game several more times (at least 5) and inspect the board each time.
expectedResults: At least some of the boards should show one or more small interior holes — empty cells completely surrounded by playable space, not touching the outer boundary.
The holes should never appear directly under the snake's starting position.
The overall board shape should still look like a single connected region with no isolated pockets of playable cells cut off from the rest.

e2eTestGuide.title: Holes grow larger and more numerous at higher Iron Snake levels
e2eTestGuide.preconditions: Have the game open in Iron Snake mode.
steps: Start an Iron Snake game and note the number and size of any interior holes on the level 1 board.
Play through several level transitions (or use any available level-select option) until you reach level 5 or higher.
After each level transition, pause and look at the new board before moving the snake.
Compare the holes on the higher-level board to what you saw at level 1.
expectedResults: Higher-level boards should have noticeably more interior holes than level 1 boards.
The holes on higher-level boards should also appear larger (covering more cells) compared to the small single-cell gaps seen early on.
No hole should ever appear directly on top of the snake or immediately in the cell the snake's head is about to enter when the new level begins.

e2eTestGuide.title: Snake dies with "Fell into a hole!" message when it enters an interior hole
e2eTestGuide.preconditions: Have the game open in Iron Snake mode with a board that has at least one visible interior hole.
steps: Start an Iron Snake game and identify a board that has an interior hole (an empty gap surrounded by playable cells).
Steer the snake so that its head moves directly into one of those interior holes.
Watch what happens the moment the head enters the hole.
expectedResults: The snake should immediately die, just as it would when hitting the outer wall.
The death message displayed on screen should read "Fell into a hole!" — not "Hit the wall!" or "Ran into itself!".
The game should then proceed to its normal end-of-game state (score display, restart option, etc.).

e2eTestGuide.title: Two-player Iron Snake: correct death message when a snake falls into a hole
e2eTestGuide.preconditions: Have the game open with two-player Iron Snake mode available.
steps: Start a two-player Iron Snake game and wait for a board with at least one visible interior hole.
Steer one of the two snakes into an interior hole while keeping the other snake alive.
Observe the death banner that appears.
expectedResults: The death banner should identify which player died and display a message such as "P1 fell into a hole" or "P2 fell into a hole" (matching whichever player entered the hole).
The message should not say "hit the wall" for a hole death.
If both snakes die simultaneously by other means (wall, self, head-on), those deaths should still show their own correct messages unchanged.
